NOTE ON CONSULTANCY FEES: Management & Technical Consultancy Fees of ₹320.50 
Lakhs paid to Choksi Trading Enterprises Pvt Ltd (a related party) are claimed as an allowable 
deduction under Section 37(1) of the Income Tax Act. These fees reduce both the book
 
profit 
and the taxable income, effectively transferring ₹320.50 Lakhs from the taxable company to 
the related party at zero tax cost for the recipient (if CTEPL is a small entity below tax 
threshold). This is the mechanism by which promoter siphoning redu
ces the tax base.

g

a

DIRECTORS' REPORT
 
 
To the Members of Arvind Polyfab Private Limited,
 
 
Your Directors have pleasure in presenting the Eleventh Annual Report of the Company together with the Audited 
Financial Statements for the financial year ended 31st March 2025.
 
 
1. Financial Highlights
 
Particulars
 
FY 2024
-
25 (₹ in Lakhs)
 
FY 2023
-
24 (₹ in Lakhs)

6,125.50
 
5,420.10

626.70

170.13

456.57
 
338.15
 
 
2. Operations and Business Review
 
During the year under review, your Company continued its operations in the manufacturing of polyester yarn and 
synthetic fabric catering to the domestic market in the states of Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Rajasthan. The total 
revenue from operations for the 
financial year 2024
-
25 stood at ₹ 6,125.50 Lakhs as against ₹ 5,420.10 Lakhs in 
the previous year, registering a growth of approximately 13.01%.
 
 
The management has continued its focus on expanding the product range towards value
-
added polyester fabrics 
and has initiated preliminary capital expenditure of ₹ 240.00 Lakhs in plant upgradation during the year. Capacity 
utilization averaged 78% across t
he manufacturing facility at Sachin GIDC.
 
 
3. Dividend
 
In view of the requirement to conserve resources for debt servicing obligations and working capital requirements, 
your Directors do not recommend any dividend for the financial year 2024
-
25.

5. Auditors

M/s Rajesh & Associates, Chartered Accountants (FRN: 124521W), Surat, were appointed as Statutory Auditors 
of the Company at the Ninth Annual General Meeting and continue to hold office. The Auditors have submitted 
their report which is annexed hereto. The
 
Board draws the attention of members to the Qualified Opinion and the 
Emphasis of Matter paragraph in the Auditor's Report and to Note 32 and Note 35 of the financial statements.
 
 
6. Acknowledgements
 
Your Directors place on record their sincere appreciation for the assistance and co
-
operation received from State 
Bank of India, HDFC Bank Limited, and other stakeholders. The Directors also thank the employees for their 
dedication during the year.
 
 
For and on behalf of the Board of Directors
 
Arvind Polyfab Private Limited

Date: 15th September 2025 | Place: Surat

MANAGEMENT DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS
 
 
1. Industry Structure and Developments
 
The Indian synthetic textile industry, particularly the polyester filament yarn and fabric segment, demonstrated 
sustained resilience during FY 2024
-
25. Domestic consumption of man
-
made fibres continued its upward 
trajectory supported by the Government's P
roduction Linked Incentive (PLI) scheme for textiles and the increasing 
preference for blended fabrics in the fast fashion and workwear segments. The Surat cluster, accounting for 
approximately 40% of India's synthetic fabric production, remained the prima
ry manufacturing hub. Industry
-
level 
capacity additions moderated during the year, providing relative pricing stability in the grey fabric segment. Global 
crude oil prices, which directly influence the cost of PTA and MEG (key raw materials for polyester),
 
remained 
range
-
bound between USD 75
-
88 per barrel, providing input cost predictability in the first half of the fiscal.
 
 
2. Opportunities and Threats
 
OPPORTUNITIES: The anticipated revision in the Free Trade Agreement with the European Union presents a 
medium
-
term export opportunity for Indian synthetic textile manufacturers. Domestic demand for technical textiles 
and performance fabrics is growing at a
pproximately 12
-
15% per annum. The Company is well
-
positioned to 
capitalize on import substitution of specialty yarns.
 
 
THREATS: The increasing cost of imported Polyester Staple Fibre (PSF) and the persistent appreciation pressure 
on input logistics costs continue to compress margins. Intense competition from unorganized sector 
manufacturers who operate outside the formal G
ST framework creates pricing distortions. Power tariff increases 
by the Gujarat state electricity board have adversely affected the cost of production by approximately 80 basis 
points during the year.
 
 
3. Risks and Concerns
 
WORKING CAPITAL AND DEBT SERVICING RISK: During the year under review, the Company 
experienced acute working capital constraints driven by protracted realization of trade 
receivables and an adverse macroeconomic environment. Consequently, there have been 
d
elays in the servicing of interest obligations to financial institutions. The management is 
actively engaged in dialogue with the consortium of lenders for a holistic restructuring of the 
debt profile to align with the projected cash flows. Additionally, t
he Company's Debt Service 
Coverage Ratio (DSCR) for the year computed at 1.18x fell below the minimum covenant 
threshold of 1.25x stipulated in the Master Facility Agreement with State Bank of India. While 
the Company has initiated discussions with the len
der for a formal waiver, such waiver had 
not been obtained as of the balance sheet date.
 
 
RAW MATERIAL PRICE RISK: Volatility in crude oil derivatives directly impacts input costs. The Company does 
not maintain formal hedging arrangements and is exposed to spot price movements in PTA and MEG.

CREDIT RISK: A significant portion of the Company's trade receivables (approximately ₹ 450 Lakhs) pertain to 
buyers in Tier 2 markets where collection cycles have elongated to 95
-
120 days during the current year, 
exacerbating liquidity pressure.
 
 
REGULATORY RISK: GST compliance requirements continue to evolve. The Company has received a scrutiny 
notice from the GST authorities for FY 2022
-
23 pertaining to ITC reconciliation differences, the outcome of which 
is currently uncertain.
 
 
4. Internal Control Systems and their Adequacy
 
The Company maintains an adequate system of internal controls commensurate with its size and nature of 
business, ensuring that all assets are safeguarded and protected against loss from unauthorized use or 
disposition. The internal audit function is conduc
ted by M/s Nilesh Shah & Co., Chartered Accountants, Surat, on 
a quarterly basis. However, the Board acknowledges that certain process improvements in the areas of 
procurement authorization and vendor master management are required and have commissioned a 
process 
improvement study during the year.
 
 
5. Human Resources and Industrial Relations
 
The Company employed 412 persons as on 31st March 2025 (Previous Year: 398 persons). Industrial relations 
remained cordial throughout the year. The Company incurred ₹ 680.50 Lakhs towards employee benefits 
expense including salaries, wages, provident fund,
 
and gratuity provisions.

INDEPENDENT AUDITOR'S REPORT
 
 
To the Members of Arvind Polyfab Private Limited
 
 
QUALIFIED OPINION
 
 
We have audited the accompanying standalone financial statements of Arvind Polyfab Private 
Limited ('the Company'), which comprise the Balance Sheet as at 31st March 2025, the Statement of 
Profit and Loss and the Cash Flow Statement for the year then ended
, and a summary of significant 
accounting policies and other explanatory information.  In our opinion and to the best of our 
information and according to the explanations given to us, EXCEPT FOR the effects of the matter 
described in the 'Basis for Qualifi
ed Opinion' section of our report, the aforesaid standalone financial 
statements give the information required by the Companies Act, 2013 ('the Act') in the manner so 
required and give a true and fair view in conformity with the accounting principles gener
ally accepted 
in India, of the state of affairs of the Company as at 31st March 2025, and its profit and cash flows for 
the year ended on that date.
 
 
BASIS FOR QUALIFIED OPINION
 
 
As detailed in Note 32 to the financial statements, the Company's Debt Service Coverage Ratio 
(DSCR) for the financial year ended March 31, 2025, computed at 0.95x on a standalone basis 
(and 1.18x on an adjusted basis inclusive of short
-
term credit lines),
 
fell below the minimum 
covenant threshold of 1.25x stipulated in the Master Facility Agreement with State Bank of 
India dated 15th April 2021. The Company had not obtained a formal waiver letter from the 
lender as of the balance sheet date of 31st March 2
025. Consequently, under the provisions of 
the loan agreement, the lender reserves the right to recall the facility. The Company has not 
reclassified the non
-
current portion of the SBI term loan amounting to ₹ 610.40 Lakhs to 
current liabilities as require
d in such circumstances. Had this reclassification been made, the 
current liabilities would have been higher by ₹ 610.40 Lakhs, the non
-
current liabilities would 
have been lower by ₹ 610.40 Lakhs, and the current ratio would have deteriorated from the 
repo
rted 1.09x to approximately 0.68x, representing a severe deterioration in the reported 
liquidity position of the Company.
 
 
EMPHASIS OF MATTER

Emphasis of Matter: We draw attention to Note 35 of the standalone financial statements, which 
describes the payment of Management and Technical Consultancy Fees aggregating to ₹ 320.50 
Lakhs to Choksi Trading Enterprises Private Limited, an enterprise ove
r which the Key Management 
Personnel exercise significant influence, and which is also a related party as defined under AS 18. 
The said entity is also a raw material supplier to the Company. The basis and commercial rationale 
for such consultancy arrangeme
nt has been noted by the Board in its resolution dated 15th April 
2024. Our opinion is not modified in respect of this matter.
 
 
REPORT ON OTHER LEGAL AND REGULATORY REQUIREMENTS
 
1. As required by the Companies (Auditor's Report) Order, 2020 ('the Order'), issued by the Central Government 
of India in terms of sub
-
section (11) of section 143 of the Companies Act, 2013, we give in the Annexure A, a 
statement on the matters specified 
in paragraphs 3 and 4 of the Order.
 
 
2. As required by Section 143(3) of the Act, we report that: (a) We have sought and obtained all the information 
and explanations which to the best of our knowledge and belief were necessary for the purposes of our audit. (b) 
In our opinion, proper books o
f account as required by law have been kept by the Company so far as it appears 
from our examination of those books. (c) The Balance Sheet, the Statement of Profit and Loss, and the Cash 
Flow Statement dealt with by this Report are in agreement with the bo
oks of account. (d) In our opinion, except for 
the matter described in the Basis for Qualified Opinion paragraph, the aforesaid financial statements comply with 
the Accounting Standards specified under Section 133 of the Act.
 
 
3. With respect to the adequacy of the internal financial controls with reference to financial statements of the 
Company and the operating effectiveness of such controls, refer to our separate Report in Annexure B.

NOTES TO THE FINANCIAL STATEMENTS
 
(All amounts in ₹ Lakhs unless otherwise stated)
 
 
NOTE 1: SIGNIFICANT ACCOUNTING POLICIES
 
1.1 Basis of Preparation: The financial statements are prepared under the historical cost convention on accrual 
basis of accounting and comply with the Accounting Standards as prescribed under Section 133 of the 
Companies Act, 2013, read with Rule 7 of the
 
Companies (Accounts) Rules, 2014 (as amended) and the 
provisions of the Companies Act, 2013. The Company is a non
-
Ind AS entity as its net worth does not exceed ₹ 
250 Crores.
 
1.2 Revenue Recognition: Revenue from sale of goods is recognised upon transfer of significant risks and 
rewards of ownership to the buyer, which generally coincides with the dispatch of goods. Revenue is stated net of 
GST, trade discounts, and returns.
 
1.3 Depreciation: Depreciation on tangible fixed assets is provided on the Written Down Value (WDV) method at 
the rates prescribed under Schedule II to the Companies Act, 2013. Depreciation for assets added/disposed 
during the year is provided on a pro
-
rat
a basis.
 
1.4 Inventories: Inventories are valued at cost or net realizable value, whichever is lower. Cost is determined on 
the FIFO (First
-
in, First
-
out) basis.
 
1.5 Borrowing Costs: Borrowing costs directly attributable to the acquisition, construction, or production of 
qualifying assets are capitalized. All other borrowing costs are charged to revenue.

-
passu charge by way of hypothecation on the entire Property, Plant and

-
directors Shri Arvind Maganlal Choksi an
d Smt. Hetal Arvind Choksi. The 
Loan Agreement stipulates a minimum Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) of 1.25x on a standalone basis.

In respect of the Income Tax demand, the Company has filed an appeal before the Commissioner of Income Tax 
(Appeals), Surat. Having received legal advice, the Board believes the claim can be successfully defended and 
that no material financial loss will be
 
incurred.
 
 
In respect of the IBC demand: The Company has received a demand notice under Section 8 of the Insolvency 
and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 from Rajkot Fibres Private Limited, an operational creditor, claiming an unpaid 
principal of ₹ 85.40 Lakhs and interest of ₹ 
22.10 Lakhs aggregating to ₹ 107.50 Lakhs. The Company has 
disputed this claim citing pre
-
existing quality deficiencies in the goods supplied. The matter is currently pending 
before the National Company Law Tribunal, Ahmedabad Bench. Management, based on p
reliminary legal 
evaluation, considers the liability to be contingent in nature and has not made any provision.
